what: what does it mean to invoke a function
how do i call a function
what is the difference between defining and calling a function
why: why does my function not run
why does passing a function with parentheses call it immediately
why does my event handler run immediately
how: how to call a function in javascript
how to invoke a method on an object
how to call a function with arguments
output: why is my callback running immediately

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Invoking (calling) a function means triggering its execution. In JavaScript there are several invocation patterns: plain function call `fn()`, method call `obj.method()`, constructor call `new Fn()`, and explicit context calls `fn.call()` / `fn.apply()`. Each pattern affects what `this` is bound to inside the function.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: Defining a function is like writing instructions on a card and filing it in a drawer. Invoking is when you pull the card out of the drawer and actually follow the instructions. Without invocation, the card just sits there forever.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When the engine evaluates a call expression like `greet('Alice')`, it: 1) resolves the reference to the function object, 2) evaluates the arguments, 3) creates a new Execution Context, 4) binds `this` based on the invocation pattern, 5) pushes the context onto the Call Stack, and 6) begins executing the function body line by line.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Arguments are copied into the new stack frame as local bindings. Primitive arguments are copied by value; object arguments copy the heap reference. Modifying an object parameter inside the function mutates the original object.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Recognising the difference between passing a function reference to a timer vs accidentally calling it immediately.

4_common_confusion.trap: Adding `()` when passing a function as a callback causes it to run immediately, and the return value (usually `undefined`) is passed instead of the function itself.
4_common_confusion.fix: When passing a function as a value (callback, event handler), write the name without parentheses: `setTimeout(announce, 2000)`. Only add `()` when you want to execute it right now.

6_edge_case.quirk: Immediately Invoked Function Expressions (IIFEs) define and invoke a function in a single expression. This was the primary scoping pattern before ES6 block scope. The `()` at the end invokes, and wrapping the whole thing in `()` makes it an expression rather than a declaration.
